title: The long draw on a spinning wheel
subtitle: Drafting a full arm's length from a carded preparation for woollen-style yarn
excerpt: The long draw drafts a full arm's length of fibre in a single stroke, using the wheel's twist to thin the fibre as it extends. It is faster than the short forward draw and produces a lofty, elastic woollen-style yarn well suited to knitting and weaving. The technique requires a carded preparation and a wheel set with light take-up.
sourceNotes: The long draw is a traditional woollen spinning technique described consistently in handspinning literature. The distinction between woollen and worsted spinning preparations and draw styles is standard. Observation-led.

content.text: yarn: lofty, elastic, and warm. Unlike the short forward draw (which produces

yarn), the long draw does not use a pinch hand to control twist. Instead, twist is allowed to enter the drafting zone, and the back hand manages how quickly and how far the fibres extend. This requires a carded preparation (rolags or a drum-carded batt) where the fibres are randomly arranged and open. Worsted preparations are too compact for the long draw.

attrs.body: Keep hair and clothing clear of the drive band and flyer. Switch the wheel off before threading or adjusting tension.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

content.text: Set the scotch tension (or double-drive brake) to the lightest

that still draws yarn in when you relax your back hand. If take-up is too strong, the bobbin pulls yarn in before the draft stroke is complete and you cannot extend the full arm's length. Treadle at a steady, moderate pace: consistent treadle speed equals consistent twist insertion.

Hold the rolag or batt end loosely in your front hand (the hand closer to the orifice). The front hand is not pinching: it guides the fibre supply without controlling the twist. The back hand rests at a comfortable distance from the orifice, holding nothing yet.

Allow some twist to enter the fibre supply through the front hand. Grip the front hand loosely enough that twist can enter the drafting zone, then begin moving the back hand away from the front hand in a slow, steady outward stroke. Twist running into the fibre zone should thin it as you extend. Continue moving the back hand until your arm is fully extended, roughly 50-70 cm.

At the end of the stroke, relax the back hand and let the wheel draw the spun length onto the bobbin. Return the back hand toward the orifice and repeat the outward stroke. The rhythm is a continuous outward draft and inward recovery. Treadle steadily throughout: slowing treadle speed during recovery inserts less twist on the new stroke.

attrs.intro: Three problems in the long draw.

items.fix: Allow more twist in before starting the stroke. Slow the outward movement of the back hand slightly.
items.cause: Either too little twist entered the drafting zone before the stroke began, or the draft extended faster than twist could support the thinning fibres.

items.fix: Prepare rolags more evenly by loading hand cards consistently. Slow the draft stroke and allow twist to catch up before extending further.
items.cause: Inconsistent drafting speed, or the rolag had uneven density.
items.symptom: Yarn is unevenly thick and thin

items.fix: Reduce scotch tension until the bobbin does not pull while the back hand is still moving outward.
items.cause: Take-up is drawing yarn onto the bobbin before the stroke is complete.
items.symptom: Cannot get a full arm's length draft

attrs.heading: Common problems
